channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Warhammer Cities of Sigmar New Units Review - To Arms!
item.content:encoded:
This week sees the release of a new batch of miniatures for the Cities of Sigmar army in Warhammer Age of Sigmar, and Games Workshop give me the chance to take a peek at the releases and their rules in the new Battletome: Cities of Sigmar. Read on for a breakdown of these Cities of Sigmar New Units, to see if these noble fighters deserve a spot on your list!

Cities of Sigmar New Units - A Closer Look

Freeguild Gallants

Stats: Move: 5”, Save: 3+, Control: 1, Health: 2

Melee Weapons: Weapons of Gallantry: Atk: 2, Hit: 3+, Wnd: 4+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: 1

Shining examples of gallantry, these stalwart sword and board warriors like to fight on foot. They have two special abilities: Not One Step Back (passive), embraces their feel of having sturdy footing, giving them a +1 bonus to their Attacks and Damage characteristics if they haven’t charged this turn.

Break Them Upon The Walls (passive), lets them boost up friendly Sigmarite units to stand tough against charge attacks, granting (Anti-charge) (+1 Rend) to any non-Gallants within 12” of them.

Play these units like the sturdy little tanks they are, setting up tough blocks against your opponents. Get them stuck into the fight, with their decent melee weapons, heightened defenses and extra health, and your enemies will have a hard time breaking through their ranks.

Freeguild Grenadiers

Stats: Move: 5”, Save: 4+, Control: 1, Health: 1

Ranged Weapons: Ruin-sweeper Arsenal: Rng: 10”, Atk: 1, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 2+, Rnd: 2, Dmg: 1

Melee Weapons: Grenadier Bardiche: Atk: 2, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 4+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: 2

You want a little more firepower in your forces? Check out the Freeguild Grenadiers, who come storming in with gouts of flame. Their special ability Cinders And Ashes (once per turn (army)) (end of any turn), lets them take aim on any terrain feature or objective being contested by an enemy unit. You roll a die, and on a 2+ you inflict mortal damage equal to the roll on that target.

To keep these dudes at their most fiery and effective, you’ll need a Cindergout (1 in ten models in this unit can be a Cindergout) to use that special ability. So keep that unit safe while you torch your enemies, and you’ll be looking like real hot stuff.

Mallus Forgepriest

Stats: Move: 5”, Save: 4+, Control: 2, Health: 5

Melee Weapons: Meteoric Warhammer (Crit (auto-wound)): Atk: 4, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 3+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: D3

Imagine a priest who walks around with a giant warhammer, blessing consecrated objectives while smashing down on your foes… that’s the Mallus Forgepriest. Along with the impressive warhammer mentioned above (4 attacks dealing D3 each is no slouch), this hero priest also have the Consecrate the Land special ability.

Once per turn during your movement phase, you can consecrate an objective you control that this unit’s contesting, if you do, other nearby units gain ward saves when on consecrated ground. It’s a cool ability, but may not move the needle enough to include this unit… unless you’re truly considering a more control-style gameplay with your Cities. In that case, Mallus Forgepriest feels like an auto-include where you hunker him down on an important objective and smash the hell out of anyone trying to mess with it.

Jorvan Kreel, Heir Of The Kraken

Stats: Move: 6”, Save: 4+, Control: 2, Health: 5

Melee Weapons: Ranger Blades: Atk: 6, Hit: 3+, Wnd: 4+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: 2

Jorvan is a noble in exile, hunting around to regain followers and his honor with his Thexa, his Ash Panther. Along with a flurry of blades (six attacks?!), he comes with a suite of three special abilities.

Prowling Ash Panther (any combat phase) lets Thexa blink in and attack an enemy unit in combat with Jorvan, rolling a D3. On a 2+, you inflict mortal damage on the target equal to the roll. If that target gets destroyed while the Ash Panther token’s next to it, you remove the token (and can do it again next time you’re in combat).

Harrying Attacks also takes advantage of the Panther token, stating “While this unit’s Thexa is next to an enemy unit, that enemy unit’s attacks cannot score critical hits (treat them as regular hits instead).

Finally, the Fluid Combat Style ability (reaction: you declared a FIGHT ability for this unit or a non-Hero Sigmarite Infantry unit wholly within 12” of this unit), allows you to blink the target unit away six inches away from an enemy after their fight ability has resolved.

He’s slippery! And that Thexa token will truly put in work. Slide him into the fray, deal lots of damage and kill your enemy units as quickly as possible, then get him moving around the battlefield fast. Think of Jorvan as a surgical blade, slipping in to strike where your opponents are most vulnerable.

Gate Gargants

Stats: Move: 8”, Save: 3+, Control: 5, Health: 10, Ward: 6+

Ranged Weapons: Scattershot Cannon: Rng: 10”, Atk: 5, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 3+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: D3

Melee Weapons: Massive Warmaul: Atk: 5, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 2+, Rnd: 2, Dmg: 3

These massive brutes can function, as the Battletome describes, as “a living gatehouse,” and just looking at them… that checks out! Their Aggressive Defenders ability (once per turn (Army)) (your movement phase) allows adjacent friendly Sigmarite units also in combat alongside them to use shoot or charge abilities even if they used a retreat action this turn, and no mortal damage is inflicted on the targets by retreat abilities for the rest of the turn.

Sounds complicated, but picture it - you’re in combat alongside these huge brutes, their mere defensive presence gives you the space to run back out of the fight, and either charge back in or fire volleys of ranged attacks!

Open The Gates is another fun ability, giving visible non-war machine Cities of Sigmar unit within 12” inches of them an additional 2 to their charge rolls. Throw these gargants into the fray, and watch your enemies struggle to move around the battlefield the way they’d like.

Erasmus Zonn, The Enlightened One

Stats: Move: 12”, Save: 4+, Control: 2, Health: 9

Ranged Weapons: Rod of Radiance (Crit (2 Hits): Rng: 10”, Atk: 2, Hit: 3+, Wnd: 3+, Rnd: 2, Dmg: D3

Melee Weapons: Glyphwing’s Heads (companion): Atk: 4, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 3+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: D3+1

The most experienced arcanist mastering Hyshian magic, Erasmus streaks across the battlefield on his Glyphwing, raining down arcane blasts from his rod of radiance while using his Lantern of All-Knowledge, which grants a bevy of benefits to choose from including shielding your friendly units from enemies, adding 1 to Erasmus’s power level, enhancing the damage of its rod of radiance, and more.

He can also target enemy magical Manifestations and turn them into his own endless spells with his Realmsphere Mastery, and bounce around corners of the battlefield with his Four Corners of the Realms spell. He’s a tricky mage that will be a huge boon to lists that run a lot of magic, but it also doesn’t hurt that he has move 12” and Fly, and can blast enemies with his ranged weapon!

Amethyst Knellmage

Stats: Move: 5”, Save: 6+, Control: 2, Health: 5

Melee Weapons: Amethyst Scythe: Atk: 3, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 4+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: D3

Unnerving, these Shyishian wizards A. Look awesome with their freaky bell-helmets and B. Can run serious interference for your army. Their Deathly Candlelight spell lets you target a visible enemy unit within 12” of this unit, and on a 7 that target can’t use commands for the rest of the turn. Imagine shutting down a very important commanding officer in your opponent’s army with this simple, freaky little mage.

Their Ominous Presence ability extends the reach of that Candlelight spell from 12” to 18”, which is basically halfway across the board! If you’re trying to run down and pick apart an enemy army, these Knellmages are an auto-include.

Aqshian Pyrocaster

Stats: Move: 5”, Save: 6+, Control: 2, Health: 5

Melee Weapons: Burning Fists: Atk: 3, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 4+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: D3

Want a little more firepower in your army? Check out these blazing spell casters! Their Incandescent Incineration spell basically lets you blast an enemy unit, dealing D3 mortal damage and making them “incandescent” (I like to translate that to: on fire), which makes the next successful cast of this spell auto deal them 3 damage. Their Flames of Aqshy ability, like the Knellblade’s Ominous Presence, can extend the range of this spell to 18”.

A much more aggressive caster, I like the thought of letting these pyromaniacs tear across the battlefield lighting enemies on fire. It just feels right, doesn’t it?

Dawner's Triumph

Stats: Move: -, Save: 4+, Control: -, Health: 8

Next up is the Dawner’s Triumph, a faction terrain for the army with some pretty cool abilities. Along with Hauled To War allowing you to basically drag this terrain across the battlefield, the Fury of the Faithful ability targets a visible enemy unit within 6” of this terrain feature, and can grant friendly Cities of Sigmar Infantry and Cavalry units the Crit (Auto-wound) ability for attacks that target the enemy unit.

I think this ability is pretty cool, if not completely devastating, but I’m really here for the battlefield presence of this terrain feature. It just looks so damn sick. And sure, it’s nice to give my units Crit (Auto-wound), but I really just like to imagine my little Sigmar dudes lugging this huge statue across the battlefield.

Cannonade Cogfort

Stats: Move: 6”, Save: 3+, Control: 10, Health: 25

Ranged Weapons: Godbreaker Cannon: Rng: 24”, Atk: 4, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 2+, Rnd: 2, Dmg: 4

Ranged Weapons: Breacher Cannon (Anit-Infantry (+1 Rend): Rng: 12”, Atk: 6, Hit: 3+, Wnd: 3+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: 2

Ranged Weapons: Crew’s Leadshotters (shoot in combat): Rng: 12”, Atk: 6, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 3+, Rnd: 1, Dmg: D3

Melee Weapons: Crushing Iron Feet (companion): Atk: 4, Hit: 4+, Wnd: 2+, Rnd: 2, Dmg: 4

Okay, did I save the best, most absurd, show-stopping unit for last? Of course I did! As the Battletome describes, “Built for total battlefield domination, the Cannonade Cogfort towers above the fray like a god of war,” …true enough!

First off, the Cogfort has an interesting “heat tokens” mechanic, where the more of its Full Power special abilities you use, the more heat tokens you gain - which could result in this unit taking damage (but with 25 health… who cares!?). That Full Power ability allows you to do many different useful abilities, like making it harder to shoot friendly nearby units, increasing its speed to 10”, re-roll shoot rolls of 1 with its Godbreaker Cannon, and shoot even after retreating.

There’s also the Special Ammunition special ability, which - if the unit’s under orders - allows you to roll a die and on a 3+ pick either Nullshot (which subtracts from ward rolls with the Godbreaker Cannon - interesting!), Heavy Shot (which adds to the Rend of the Godbreaker), or Grapeshot (which auto-deals D3 mortal damage to adjacent enemy units if you only targeted one unit with the Godbreaker).

A towering presence on the battlefield, and a testament to Games Workshop’s willingness to get inventive, creative, and weird, the Cannonade Cogfort is, in my opinion, kind of a masterpiece! Do I need to tell you how to play a giant walking cannon? Just stomp and shoot, stomp and shoot.

Cities of Sigmar New Units - Final Thoughts

While not every one of these new units deserves a place on every list, there’s absolutely something for every Cities of Sigmar player. Whether you’re focusing on area control, heavy offense, a magic-heavy list, or just want to overwhelm your opponents with sheer firepower, I think this is one of the strongest new batches of units we’ve seen in a very long time!

The Cities of Sigmar New Units used in the creation of this review were provided by Games Workshop. All photos courtesy Games Workshop.



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/warhammer-cities-of-sigmar-new-units-review-to-arms
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Thyrielle's Zephyrites Warband Review - High Fey Fighters Show Off Their Abilities
item.content:encoded:
A new Warhammer Underworlds Warband has been revealed: Thyrielle's Zephyrites. This four-fighter warband uses the power of wind to empower their movement, abilities, and even utilizes a few 'once per game' abilities.

Thyrielle's Zephyrites Are A Gorgeous Warband

Thyrielle is the master of the Hurakan temple, travelling with her air aelementor warband to fight against the forces of evil. Key to her journey is the fox-like spirit, Tzul, who guides her way and empowers her. The rest of the warband uses the wind to strengthen their resolve, or in the case of their blind archer, to guide his arrows to their targets.

Before delving into their unique abilities and the gameplay of this warband, I have to stop and highlight just how good this group looks. The warband consists of Thyrielle, the leader, Orieth, the blind archer, and acolytes Serikith and Anara. In addition to the four fighters, there is also a miniature for the fox spirit Tzul.

With light blues and light golden robes, these characters embody the wind that they channel as their source of power. This theme of wind is shown most heavily in the leader Thyrielle, with a large fan in one hand, and I'm not entirely sure what's in the other hand but along with her cape it looks to be billowing in the wind.

There are a lot of Warbands that I'll look at and think they look very cool, or the detailing is beautifully intricate, and I'm impressed at the modeling work that went into them. From the moment I picked up this box, I knew I had to put these minis together and paint them.

A Windy Warband In Action

The core mechanic of this Warband has to do with the placement of each fighter relative to the fox spirit, Tzul.

At the start of each Action Step, Tzul will get placed on the Battlefield in an empty hex that doesn't contain a feature token. Then, each of the hexes out from Tzul in straight lines up to the edge of the board or a blocked hex is reached, that space becomes Windblow. With the end of every turn and the start of a new Action Step, you'll be picking up Tzul and placing them back down, causing new tiles to become Windblow.

Each of the members of Thyrielle's Zephyrites can then use the Windblown status in different ways. With the Zephyr Dance ability, friendly fighters who are Windblown with any more and or charge tokens have +1 save dice, Zephyr Leap lets friendly fighters who start in a Windblown space have flying and +1 move, and Hurrican Aid means that Enemy Windblown fighters are flanked.

A Fighter making a successful attack while Windblown is also inspired. With a chance to get a lot of characters in a Windblown hex early on and into combat, you could very quickly take an aggressive stance.

Each of these abilities on its own is a good friendly boon or helpful enemy bane, but what will separate the good players from the great players who use this Warband is how many of these situations they can pull off in a single turn.

Early in the game, a chance for Zephyr Leap to trigger on all of your fighters can set up advantageous plays and movement. As the fighters of each side begin to intermingle a bit more, you might be able to empower some characters to move out of danger while setting up defensive plays for healthier reinforcements to come in and take the brunt of some damage.

The Zephyrites also have two abilities that can only be used once per game. These abilities really focus on the movement and strategic placement of units around the board, but can let you quickly give yourself a better board state or use it to make a surprise antagonistic push towards your opponent.

One With The Wind lets you move Tzul once during the Power Step as long as your Leader is also on the battlefield. While Cyclonic Pull will let you pick a Windblown Fighter and move them towards Tzul for as many or as few spaces as you want. If you're trying to get away from someone or something, you can retreat. Alternatively, if you want a fighter to move into an advantageous position, they can close in when they might not have normally been able to.

Thyrielle's Zephyrites Review | Final Thoughts

For those who enjoy strategizing exactly where their fighters will be and guessing where your opponent is about to put their fighters, Thyrielle's Zephyrites is a really fun Warband to be playing.

They are quick to inspire with a unique ability that will also force your opponent to watch their lines as they question where Tzul will appear next.

This definitely isn't a Warband that's going to survive on just moving forward and hitting hard, but the mobility and power benefits of being in the right place at the right time will have a chance to quickly outmaneuver your opponents.

Thyrielle's Zephyrites were reviewed with a copy provided by Games Workshop - All images were taken over the course of the review

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/wh-underworlds-thyrielles-zephyrites-warband-review-high-fey-fighters-show-off
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
